rulings.Case Title: KHP III SUTTER LLC A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY VS. ASSESMENT APPEALS BOARD FOR THE CITY AND COUNTY OF
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 4, 2019, 2018, Line 1, PETITIONER KHP III SUTTER LLC A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY's Motion For Attorneys Fees. Judge Schulman discloses that he is professionally acquainted with Don Willenburg, one of Petitioner's counsel. This will not affect his ability to fairly and impartially decide this matter. Petitioner KHP III SF Sutter LLC's motion for an award of attorneys' fees under Code of Civil Procedure section 1021.5 is denied. While a taxpayer's due process right to appeal from a real property tax assessment undoubtedly is an important right affecting the public interest, the necessity and financial burden of private enforcement are not such as to make the requested award appropriate. "It is within the trial court's discretion to deny attorneys' fees pursuant to section 1021.5 on the ground that the plaintiff's personal stake in the outcome was not disproportionate to the burden of private enforcement, even where the litigation enforced an important right and conferred a significant benefit upon the public." (Satrap v. Pacific Gas & Electric Co. (1996) 42 Cal.App.4th 72, 78 [collecting authorities].) KHP bears the burden of establishing that its litigation costs transcend its personal interest. (Beach Colony II v. California Coastal Comm'n (1985) 166 Cal.App.3d 106, 112-114.) It has not carried that burden. Petitioner had a very substantial financial interest in filing an administrative appeal from a $44.6 million supplemental assessment. Petitioner's position is that the property should have been assessed at $30.6 million, which if accepted would result in very substantially lower property taxes. (Dressman Dec., Ex. A, Admin. Record, pp. 3, 71.) Thus, KHP's personal financial stake in the outcome was not so disproportionate to the cost of the litigation (for which it seeks a total of $55,961.50 in attorneys' fees) that the writ of mandate proceeding would not have been brought without the additional incentive of an award of attorneys' fees. Petitioner's motion for an award of attorneys' fees in the amount of $7,500 under Government Code section 800 is granted. The Board's dismissal of Petitioner's appeal as untimely was arbitrary and capricious. An agency's decision may be found to be arbitrary and capricious when no substantial evidence supports the agency's actions or where there has been " 'a stubborn insistence on following an unauthorized course of action.' " (Campbell v. Residential Rent Stabilization and Arbitration Board (1983) 142 Cal.App.3d 123, 128-129.) Both conditions apply here. In granting the writ, the Court explicitly found that "[t]he Board's determination that KHP's reliance on the language in the Instructions was unreasonable constitutes prejudicial abuse of discretion because it is not supported by substantial evidence in the administrative record." Further, the Court found that the Board was equitably estopped from rejecting KHP's appeal of the supplemental assessment because it affirmatively advised KHP that it could file an appeal from the supplemental assessment within 60 days of the tax bill, and then refused to consider that appeal. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for petitioners is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: NOELLE BONNER VS. ALEX KWAK
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing
rulings.Rulings: Appearances: Jay Song, Esq., Logos professional , appearing for Plaintiff , appearing for Defendant No other appearance. Set for hearing on Monday, February 4, 2019, 2018, Line 2, PETITIONER NOELLE BONNER's Notice Of Hearing. Petitioners Noelle Bonner and The Nobo, LLC's petition to confirm the amended final arbitration award dated November 15, 2018 is granted, and Respondents Alex Kwak and Applepalm Enterprises, Inc.'s petition to vacate that award is denied. The Court will enter judgment confirming the award in favor of The Nobo, LLC and against Respondent Applepalm Enterprises, Inc. per Code of Civil Procedure section 1287.4. The scope of judicial review of arbitration awards is extremely narrow. An arbitrator's decision generally is not reviewable for errors of fact or law. (Moncharsh v. Heily & Blase (1992) 3 Cal.4th 1, 11; Branches Neighborhood Corp. v. Calatlantic Group, Inc. (2018) 26 Cal.App.5th 743, 750.) Respondents have not shown either that the arbitrator exceeded his powers or that their rights were substantially prejudiced by the arbitrator's refusal to postpone the hearing upon sufficient cause being shown therefor or by his refusal to hear evidence material to the controversy. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1286.2(a)(4),(5).) The arbitrator's award of damages bore a rational relationship to the contract and the breach found by the arbitrator. (Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. v. Intel Corp. (1994) 9 Cal.4th 362, 381; San Francisco Housing Authority v. Service Employees Internat. Union, Local 790 (2010) 182 Cal.App.4th 933, 943-944.) Respondents have not shown that the arbitrator refused to continue the hearing after the date for the hearing was set. Nor have Respondents offered any admissible evidence to contradict the arbitrator's express finding that he considered the evidence offered at the hearing, which included Respondents' proffered evidence. rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, February 4, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFFs JUVENAL VIRRUETE, JOAQUIN DELGADO, and DANIEL GONZALES' Motion For Terminating Sanctions Against Defendants 724 Ent, Inc. And Jason Wanigatunga For Willfully Failing To Comply With Court Order Pro Tem Judge Chuck Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted, no opposition. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to cgeerhart@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Second Deposition Of Plaintiff Jason Roussos And To Permit Deposition After Discovery Cut-Off
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, February 4, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT POUR GUYS SOMA LLC DBA TEMPEST BAR & GRILL's Motion To Compel Second Deposition Of Plaintiff Jason Roussos And To Permit Deposition After Discovery Cut-Off Pro Tem Judge Chuck Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted. The second deposition of plaintiff shall be limited in duration to three hours (combined for all defendants). The scope of deposition questioning is limited to: treatment for injuries from the stabbing incident by those health care providers who were previously undisclosed by plaintiff in response to an interrogatory calling for identification of said health care provider. To be clear: this second deposition is not an opportunity to update any prior questioning or go beyond questioning regarding newly discovered treaters. The Court further orders that inquiry at deposition into treatment unrelated to the incident is barred under Britt v. rulings.Case Title: JASON ROUSSOS VS. POUR GUYS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Cms Security, Incorporated'S Motion For Summary Judgment. (Part 2 Of 2) For Tentative Ruling Only
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 4, 2019, 2018, Line 4, Defendant Cms Security, Incorporated'S Motion For Summary Judgment. (Part 2 Of 2) (For Tentative Ruling Only.) CMS fails to meet its burden of production on the causation issue. CMS merely shows that Mr. Be-Caamal was the direct cause of the injury. (CMS' Undisputed Fact 2.) There exists a triable issue of fact regarding whether CMS' inaction was a substantial factor in causing Mr. Rousso's injury. (See Mukthar v. Latin American Security (2006) 139 Cal.App.4th 284, 291 [question of fact whether presence of armed security guard would have prevented assault].) rulings.Case Title: JASON ROUSSOS VS. POUR GUYS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 4, 2019, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT CMS SECURITY, INCORPORATED's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. (part 1 of 2) Defendant CMS Security, Inc.'s ("CMS") motion for summary judgment is denied. CMS fails to demonstrate as a matter of law that it did not owe a duty of care to Mr. Roussos. Businesses, such as restaurants and bars, have an affirmative duty to take reasonable steps to secure their premises against reasonably foreseeable criminal acts of third parties. (Delgado v. Trax Bar & Grill (2005) 36 Cal.4th 224, 235.) Under certain circumstances, this special-relationship-based duty may include a duty to provide security guards to protect the safety of patrons. (Id. at 236-240.) In this case, The Tempest Bar & Grill Restaurant ("Tempest") hired CMS to provide security services for its restaurant. As CMS concedes, a duty of care may extend to property beyond that owned or possessed by the defendant, and the dispositive issue is whether the defendant exercised control over the adjacent property. (CMS Opposing Points & Authorities, 12:21-23.) CMS does not unequivocally establish its absence of control over the area where the stabbing occurred. In determining whether the proprietor exercised sufficient control over such areas that a duty should be imposed, courts examine such factors as (1) whether customers and invitees regularly use the adjacent property (such as a parking lot or, here, a public sidewalk); (2) whether defendant was aware of this use; (3) whether the adjacent area was directly in front of the bar or restaurant's windows; (4) whether defendant's lease authorized the nonexclusive use of the adjacent property for use by customers and invitees; (5) whether a reasonable inference can be drawn that defendant realized a significant commercial benefit from his customers' use of the adjacent property. (Morris v. De La Torre (2005) 36 Cal.4th 260, 274.) There is a disputed issue of material fact regarding whether the stabbing occurred on property within CMS's control, and the Court therefore denies the motion for summary judgment. (Southland Corp. v. Superior Court (1988) 203 Cal.App.3d 656, 662-663 [triable issues of fact existed as to proprietor's actual or apparent control of property adjacent to business premises which was neither owned nor possessed by the proprietor].) Tempest runs the "Box Kitchen." The fight occurred on the sidewalk by the kitchen. Patrons order their food from the kitchen's window and wait on the sidewalk while it is being prepared. (Bright Depo., 33:1-6). Tempest's lease provides that it agreed to clean the exterior sidewalks. (Braun Dec., Ex. 8, par. 27). Part of Mr. Lopez's security duties was to shoo away homeless persons that slept on the sidewalk and/or hassled kitchen patrons. (Lopez Depo., 48:21-49:2; 51:3-8; 53:11-21.) Tempest even had a bench on the sidewalk. (Lopez Depo., 190:16-191:5.) Mr. Roussos further shows that CMS' "Tempest Door Checklist" states that CMS should watch for misconduct "outside" and "near the Box window." (Braun Dec., Ex. 11.) Lastly, CMS' own guidelines contemplate its outside presence. (Braun Dec., Ex. 7) These facts distinguish CMS' cases. In those cases, there was no evidence that the defendant controlled the adjacent property. (See Medina v. Hillshore Partners (1995) 40 Cal.App.4th 477, 483 ["The negligence and premises liability causes of action fail because no facts are alleged that the decedent . . . was assaulted on property controlled by landowner"]; Balard v. Bassment Event Security, Inc. (1989) 210 Cal.App.3d 243, 247-248; Owen v. Kings Super Market (1988) 198 Cal.App.3d 379, 385-386.) (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KAREN ELAINE GOODRICH MD VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 4, 2019, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANT MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Medical Board of California's demurrer to Plaintiff's second amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend on the grounds that it fails to state facts sufficient to constitute any cause of action. (Code Civ. Proc. § 340.) The Court sustained the Medical Board's previous demurrers in part without leave to amend, but granted Plaintiff leave to amend her claim that the Medical Board has violated Business and Professions Code Section 828 by not excluding investigative reports from information provided to persons outside of the Medical Board. Plaintiff's second amended complaint does not allege any facts showing any breach of confidentiality of the records contained within the Medical Board's investigative files, nor has she alleged which such records were supposedly disclosed or provided by the Medical Board, when, or to whom. Nor has Plaintiff met her burden to demonstrate how the complaint can be amended to state a valid cause of action. rulings.Case Title: CRAIG K MARTIN VS. LEAH T WILSON
rulings.Calendar Matter: Special Motion And Special Motion To Strike Plaintiff Verified Complaint For Damages
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 4, 2019, 2018, Line 13, DEFENDANT STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA's Special Motion To Strike Plaintiff Verified Complaint For Damages. Defendant The State Bar of California's special motion to strike Plaintiff Craig K. Martin's complaint is granted. Defendant prevails on both prongs of the anti-SLAPP motion analysis. First, Defendant has shown that the course of action challenged by Plaintiff arises from protected petitioning or free speech activity. As Plaintiff concedes in his opposition, his claims arise entirely from the allegedly false statement about his failure to pass the California Professional Responsibility Exam (CPRE) by February 15, 1993 as required by the State Bar and the resulting suspension of his legal license. (See, e.g., Verified Complaint 5, 6, 43, 48, 54, 60, 67, 74, 80, 82, 84.) The publication of Plaintiff's suspension from legal practice on April 23, 1993 on the online State Bar Profile constitutes a "writing made in connection with an issue under consideration or review by a . . . judicial body, or any other official proceeding authorized by law" because it related to the judicial proceedings of the State Bar Court in issuing Plaintiff's suspension. (Code Civ. Proc. §425.16(e)(2); see also Barry v. State Bar of California (2017) 2 Cal.5th 318, 322 [detailing reasoning of trial court that State Bar Court proceedings arise from protected petitioning activities for purposes of the anti-SLAPP statute].) Regarding the second prong of the anti-SLAPP test, Plaintiff has not established a reasonable probability of prevailing on the merits of his claims as required by Code of Civil Procedure section 425.16(b)(1), (2). As an initial matter, Plaintiff has failed to put forth any evidence in support of his allegation that the information published on the online State Bar Profile is false and defamatory. He has therefore failed to meet his burden on proof on the second prong. Moreover, Defendant has provided ample evidence to show that no false or defamatory statement was published on Plaintiff's State Bar Profile. Finally, Plaintiff cannot show a reasonable probability of prevailing on the merits of his claims because he previously brought a substantially identical claim, which Court dismissed by order dated December 9, 2015 granting defendants' special motion to strike; that order is res judicata. Accordingly, Plaintiff's complaint is dismissed with prejudice as to all Defendants. Defendant The State Bar of California is granted leave to file a motion for attorneys' fees and costs incurred in defense of Plaintiff's lawsuit per Code of Civil Procedure 425.16(c). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order and judgment which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CRAIG K MARTIN VS. LEAH T WILSON
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 4, 2019, 2018, Line 13, DEFENDANT STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant The State Bar of California's demurrer is off calendar as moot per the court's ruling on the Anti-SLAPP motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CRAIG K MARTIN VS. LEAH T WILSON
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 4, 2019, 2018, Line 13, DEFENDANT STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendant The State Bar of California's motion to strike portions of Plaintiff Craig K. Martin's complaint is off calendar as moot per the court's ruling on the Anti-SLAPP motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/EPS)
